title: Intersecting arrays

content.problemStatement:
Given two integer arrays nums1nums1nums1 and nums2nums2nums2, return an array of their intersection. Each element in the result must appear as many times as it shows in both arrays and return the result in **sorted order**.

Intersection is defined as the common element in both arrays. For example -

nums1nums1nums1 - [1, 2] and nums2nums2nums2 - [2, 1, 3]

Therefore, only 1 and 2 are common in both arrays. On sorting the resultant array would be [1, 2].


Input Format

The first line contains two integers nnn , mmm— the size of nums1nums1nums1 and nums2nums2nums2.
The second line contains n integers a1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,ana1,a2,…,an — the number of nums1nums1nums1.
The third line contains m integers b1,b2,…,bmb1,b2,…,bmb1,b2,…,bm — the number of nums2nums2nums2.


Output Format
Print all the elements that are appearing in both the arrays in sorted order.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers n and m — the sizes of the two arrays. The second line contains n integers — the elements of the first array. The third line contains m integers — the elements of the second array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the intersection of the two arrays. Elements must appear as many times as they occur in both arrays and must be printed in non‑decreasing order, separated by a single space. If there is no common element, output an empty line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Both arrays contain 1 and 2. After sorting the intersection is [1, 2].
Only the number 3 appears in both arrays.
The value 1 appears twice in both arrays, so it is printed twice.

solution.approach: Sort both arrays and use the two‑pointer technique. After sorting, walk through the arrays with indices i and j. If a[i] == b[j], the element belongs to the intersection; record it and advance both pointers. If a[i] < b[j] advance i, otherwise advance j. Because the arrays are sorted, this yields the intersection in non‑decreasing order and respects the required multiplicities. The overall complexity is dominated by the sorting step.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n log n + m log m) per test case, where n and m are the sizes of the two arrays.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) for the sorted arrays (in‑place sorting) plus O(k) for the output, where k is the size of the intersection.

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i: test case index (0 <= i <= 20)
    # RINT(a,b): random integer in [a,b]
    # POWER(a,b): a**b
    if i == 0:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        n = m = 1
        print(n, m)
        print(0)
        print(0)
    elif i == 1:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        n = m = 1
        print(n, m)
        print(0)
        print(1)
    # Add further generation logic for other indices as needed.
